modifiedDandyCriteria: Symptoms and signs of elevated intracranial pressure (headache, papilledema, visual disturbances)
Elevated lumbar puncture opening pressure >250 mm CSF (measured with patient in lateral decubitus position)
Normal CSF composition (no pleocytosis, normal protein and glucose)
No evidence of hydrocephalus, mass lesion, structural abnormality, or meningeal enhancement on neuroimaging
No other identifiable cause of intracranial hypertension (e.g., medications, venous sinus thrombosis, systemic disease)
visualOutcomeScales: Humphrey visual field perimetry: mean deviation (MD) for quantifying visual field loss
Papilledema grading: Frisen scale Grade 0 (normal) to Grade 5 (severe papilledema)
Best corrected visual acuity (BCVA): Snellen or LogMAR
Optical coherence tomography: retinal nerve fiber layer thickness and total retinal thickness

severityStratification.features: Headache only, mild papilledema (Frisen 1-2), normal visual fields, opening pressure 250-350 mm CSF
Headache plus transient visual obscurations, moderate papilledema (Frisen 3-4), visual field defects but normal central acuity, opening pressure 350-450 mm CSF
Persistent visual loss, severe papilledema (Frisen 5), visual field constriction, central acuity loss, opening pressure >450 mm CSF; fulminant IIH with rapid vision loss over days

historyFeatures.typicalPresentation: Headache in obese young woman of childbearing age, accompanied by transient visual obscurations, pulsatile tinnitus, and diplopia
headacheCharacteristics: Retro-orbital or bifrontal, may be hemicranial
Worse in morning or after lying flat
May be aggravated by Valsalva maneuver, bending over, or coughing
Often described as pressure-like or band-like
visualSymptoms: Transient visual obscurations: brief (seconds) episodes of blackout or gray-out of vision in one or both eyes, often with postural change
Blurred vision: may be persistent or intermittent
Visual field loss: peripheral constriction, enlarged blind spot, arcuate defects, nasal steps
Central vision loss: late sign, indicates severe or prolonged papilledema
Diplopia: usually horizontal, due to non-localizing sixth nerve (abducens) palsy from elevated ICP
associatedSymptoms: Pulsatile tinnitus: whooshing sound synchronous with heartbeat, often unilateral or bilateral
Radicular pain in arms (from compressed nerve roots due to elevated CSF pressure in spinal canal)
Cognitive symptoms: difficulty concentrating, memory impairment
naturalHistory: May be self-limited or chronic with relapses and remissions
Without treatment, 10-25% develop significant permanent visual loss
Weight loss is strongly associated with disease remission
Resolution of papilledema lags behind symptom improvement by weeks to months

ophthalmologicExam: Papilledema: optic disc swelling, usually bilateral but may be asymmetric
Frisen grading of papilledema: Grade 0 to 5 based on degree of disc elevation, peripapillary halo, vessel obscuration, and nerve fiber layer hemorrhage
Enlarged blind spot on confrontation or formal perimetry
Visual field defects: peripheral constriction, nasal steps, arcuate scotomas
Reduced visual acuity: late sign, indicates optic nerve damage
Afferent pupillary defect: if asymmetric optic nerve damage
Abducens nerve palsy: unilateral or bilateral sixth nerve palsy (non-localizing sign of elevated ICP)
neurologicExam: Cranial nerves: may show unilateral or bilateral abducens palsy
No focal motor, sensory, or cerebellar deficits
Normal level of consciousness (patient is alert)
No meningeal signs (unless low CSF volume syndrome from prior LP)
fundoscopicFeatures: Frisen Grade 1: C-shaped halo of peripapillary edema, obscures temporal disc border
Frisen Grade 2: circumferential halo, elevation of nasal border
Frisen Grade 3: elevation of entire disc, obscured major vessels at disc margin
Frisen Grade 4: marked elevation, obscured major vessels on disc surface
Frisen Grade 5: dome-shaped protrusion, complete vessel obscuration, central cup absent
Peripapillary hemorrhages, exudates, cotton wool spots in severe acute papilledema
Optic atrophy in chronic papilledema (disc pallor indicates irreversible damage)

riskFactors: Obesity: strongly associated, especially central/abdominal obesity; >90% of patients are overweight or obese
Female sex: female to male ratio 8:1
Recent weight gain: even modest weight gain may precipitate onset
Endocrine factors: polycystic ovary syndrome, hyperandrogenism, hypothyroidism, Cushing disease
Medications: tetracycline and minocycline, vitamin A and retinoids, nalidixic acid, nitrofurantoin, lithium, tamoxifen, growth hormone, corticosteroids (or corticosteroid withdrawal)
Venous sinus abnormalities: bilateral transverse sinus stenosis, jugular venous compression, venous sinus thrombosis (excluded by modified Dandy criteria)
Sleep apnea: obesity hypoventilation and obstructive sleep apnea may increase ICP
Anemia: iron deficiency anemia associated with IIH in some case series
Renal disease: chronic kidney disease, polycystic kidney disease

subtypes.name: Primary IIH (Pseudotumor Cerebri Syndrome)
subtypes.description: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension without identifiable cause meeting modified Dandy criteria
subtypes.details: Classic phenotype: obese women of childbearing age, no identifiable secondary cause; management focuses on weight loss, acetazolamide, and serial visual monitoring

subtypes.name: Secondary Intracranial Hypertension
subtypes.description: Intracranial hypertension due to identifiable cause (not truly idiopathic)
subtypes.details: Causes include: cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, medications (tetracycline, vitamin A, lithium, tamoxifen, growth hormone), dural arteriovenous fistula, arteriovenous malformation, endocrine disorders, chronic meningitis, obstructive sleep apnea

subtypes.name: Fulminant IIH
subtypes.description: Rapidly progressive vision loss over days to weeks with severe papilledema
subtypes.details: Ophthalmologic emergency; requires aggressive treatment: high-dose acetazolamide, serial LPs, corticosteroids, and urgent surgical intervention (optic nerve sheath fenestration or CSF shunting) if no improvement

subtypes.name: IIH Without Papilledema
subtypes.description: Elevated ICP without optic disc swelling but with other symptoms (headache, pulsatile tinnitus, diplopia)
subtypes.details: Diagnosis of exclusion; must exclude all secondary causes; controversial entity; requires careful ophthalmologic assessment and documented elevated opening pressure

mimics: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis: may mimic IIH exactly; requires dedicated MR venography to exclude
Chronic meningitis: infectious (TB, fungal, cryptococcal, Lyme) or inflammatory (sarcoidosis, SLE); CSF analysis shows pleocytosis or abnormal protein
Mass lesion: brain tumor, abscess, subdural hematoma; excluded by MRI with contrast
Dural arteriovenous fistula: may present with headache and pulsatile tinnitus; MR venography and angiography needed
Spontaneous intracranial hypotension: orthostatic headache worse upright, low opening pressure on LP; MRI with pachymeningeal enhancement, brain sag
Hydrocephalus (obstructive or communicating): ventricular enlargement on imaging; excluded by MRI
Obstructive sleep apnea: can cause morning headache and elevated ICP; sleep study required
Endocrine disorders: Cushing disease, hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, Addison disease, hypoparathyroidism
Medication-induced intracranial hypertension: thorough medication history essential
Superior vena cava syndrome: bilateral arm swelling, facial edema, neck vein distention
Systemic lupus erythematosus with cerebral venous involvement

bedside: Fundoscopic examination: detailed papilledema grading using Frisen scale
Visual acuity: Snellen chart at distance, near card
Color vision: Ishihara color plates (often affected early in optic neuropathy)
Pupillary examination: check for relative afferent pupillary defect
Confrontation visual fields: screen for peripheral constriction; limited sensitivity
Extraocular movements: assess for sixth nerve palsy
Blood pressure measurement: rule out hypertensive urgency/emergency
laboratory: CSF analysis: opening pressure (lateral decubitus), cell count, glucose, protein, culture, cytology; normal composition required for modified Dandy criteria
Prothrombin time, aPTT, D-dimer, factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene mutation: if venous thrombosis suspected
Complete blood count: screen for anemia
Serum electrolytes, BUN, creatinine: baseline for acetazolamide therapy
Thyroid function tests: TSH, free T4 to screen for hypothyroidism
Vitamin A and retinol levels: if retinoid toxicity suspected
ESR, CRP, ANA, anti-Ro/La: if inflammatory cause suspected
HIV, syphilis (VDRL/FTA-ABS): if infectious cause suspected
Pregnancy test: before initiating acetazolamide (pregnancy category C)

imaging.modality: MRI brain with and without contrast (mandatory)
imaging.details: Essential to exclude mass lesion, hydrocephalus, and other structural causes; perform before LP if mass lesion suspected
findings: Empty sella turcica: pituitary gland compressed by CSF pressure (partial or complete empty sella)
Flattened posterior globes: bilateral flattening of sclera at optic nerve insertion
Distended optic nerve sheaths with tortuosity
Transverse sinus stenosis: bilateral or dominant-side stenosis common

imaging.details: Essential to exclude cerebral venous sinus thrombosis as cause of intracranial hypertension
findings: Transverse sinus stenosis: smooth tapering or filling defects
Venous sinus thrombosis: filling defect within sinus, often with associated cortical vein thrombosis
Hypoplastic sinuses: congenital variant, may be normal

imaging.modality: CT head with contrast (if MRI unavailable or contraindicated)
imaging.details: Less sensitive than MRI for identifying underlying causes
imaging.findings: Empty sella, small ventricles, flattened globes; cannot exclude venous sinus thrombosis adequately

imaging.details: Alternative to MRV if MRI contraindicated; better for bony detail
imaging.findings: Venous sinus filling defects, transverse sinus stenosis

imaging.details: Diagnostic gold standard; must be performed in lateral decubitus position with legs extended; position patient relaxed with neck extended
findings: Opening pressure >250 mm CSF (upper limit of normal 200-250 mm CSF in obese patients)
Normal CSF composition: WBC <5, protein <45 mg/dL, glucose normal
CSF removal may provide therapeutic benefit (temporary symptom relief)
imaging.contraindications: Any suspicion of mass lesion or herniation on imaging

imaging.details: Quantitative measure of retinal nerve fiber layer and total retinal thickness; used to monitor response to treatment
imaging.findings: Increased peripapillary RNFL thickness in acute papilledema; thinning in chronic papilledema indicates optic atrophy

imaging.modality: Formal visual field perimetry (Humphrey or Goldmann)
imaging.details: Essential for baseline and serial monitoring to detect visual deterioration
imaging.findings: Enlarged blind spot, peripheral constriction, arcuate defects, nasal step; mean deviation quantifies global field loss

management.protocolReference: Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension Treatment Trial (IIHTT) and AAN Practice Guideline (2018)

medicalManagement.dose: Start 500 mg twice daily, titrate to 1000-2000 mg twice daily or maximum tolerated dose
medicalManagement.mechanism: Carbonic anhydrase inhibitor; reduces CSF production by choroid plexus
medicalManagement.monitoring: Serum electrolytes, BUN, creatinine, bicarbonate (metabolic acidosis); monitor for paresthesias, taste alteration, nausea
medicalManagement.adverseEffects: Peripheral paresthesias (common), metallic taste, nausea, diarrhea, metabolic acidosis, hypokalemia, nephrolithiasis (long-term); contraindicated in sulfa allergy (cross-reactivity controversial but caution advised)

medicalManagement.dose: Start 25 mg twice daily, titrate to 100-200 mg twice daily
medicalManagement.mechanism: Carbonic anhydrase inhibitor plus weight loss benefit; alternative if acetazolamide not tolerated
medicalManagement.monitoring: CBC, LFTs, serum bicarbonate; monitor for cognitive side effects, nephrolithiasis, angle-closure glaucoma
medicalManagement.adverseEffects: Cognitive impairment (word-finding difficulty), weight loss, paresthesias, metabolic acidosis, nephrolithiasis
medicalManagement.note: Dual benefit of reducing ICP and promoting weight loss

medicalManagement.dose: 20-40 mg daily to twice daily (adjunctive therapy)
medicalManagement.note: Second- or third-line adjunctive agent; limited evidence of efficacy

medicalManagement.dose: Target 5-15% reduction in total body weight
medicalManagement.details: Most effective long-term treatment; 6-10% weight loss can lead to papilledema resolution; bariatric surgery effective in refractory obesity
medicalManagement.evidence: IIHTT showed 6% weight loss resulted in improved visual fields and papilledema grade

surgicalManagement.indications: Progressive visual loss despite maximal medical therapy; fulminant IIH with acute vision loss; poor visual acuity at presentation
surgicalManagement.procedure: Unilateral or bilateral fenestration of optic nerve sheath to allow CSF filtration and decompress optic nerve
surgicalManagement.success: Effective in halting or improving visual loss in 80-90%
surgicalManagement.risks: Transient diplopia, optic nerve injury, infection, hemorrhage, failure (requires repeat surgery or shunt)

surgicalManagement.indications: Refractory headache and elevated ICP when visual loss not the primary indication; failed ONSF; combined headache and visual deterioration
surgicalManagement.preferred: Ventriculoperitoneal shunt preferred over lumboperitoneal (lower rate of shunt failure and revision)
surgicalManagement.success: Effective for ICP control and headache improvement
surgicalManagement.risks: Shunt failure (30-50% within 2 years requiring revision), infection, overdrainage (low-pressure headache, subdural hematoma), shunt obstruction, abdominal complications

surgicalManagement.indications: Select patients with focal transverse sinus stenosis and pressure gradient >8-10 mm Hg on venography
surgicalManagement.procedure: Endovascular stent placement across stenotic transverse sinus segment
surgicalManagement.success: 70-80% improvement of headache and papilledema in carefully selected patients
surgicalManagement.risks: Stent thrombosis, vessel perforation, restenosis, intracranial hemorrhage, headache recurrence
surgicalManagement.note: Requires specialized neurointerventional expertise; not first-line

serialLumbarPunctures: Indications: temporary measure while awaiting surgery or medication effect; fulminant IIH
Goal: remove CSF until closing pressure approximately half of opening pressure
Limitations: temporary effect (CSF reaccumulates within hours), painful, risk of CSF leak, infection, not practical for long-term management
corticosteroids: Use: controversial; may be considered in fulminant IIH as temporizing measure
Dose: dexamethasone 4-8 mg IV or oral daily or methylprednisolone 500-1000 mg IV daily
Warning: weight gain from high-dose steroids may worsen underlying condition; not for chronic use
pathways: Mild-moderate IIH: start acetazolamide, titrate to 2-4 g daily; initiate weight loss program with dietitian referral; monitor visual fields and OCT monthly initially
Severe IIH: high-dose acetazolamide, urgent ophthalmology and neurosurgery consultation; consider ONSF or shunt urgently if visual loss progresses
Fulminant IIH: hospital admission, IV acetazolamide, serial LPs, high-dose IV methylprednisolone, urgent ONSF or shunt within days
Long-term: weight loss target 5-15%, continued medical therapy, wean acetazolamide after papilledema resolved; monitor every 3-6 months for recurrence

pathophysiology.primaryMechanism: Elevated intracranial pressure due to impaired CSF absorption or increased CSF production, in the absence of a mass lesion or hydrocephalus
CSFDynamics: Increased CSF pressure: opening pressure consistently >250 mm CSF
Impaired CSF absorption: reduced CSF outflow conductance at arachnoid granulations
Increased cerebral venous pressure: elevated sagittal sinus pressure leads to increased resistance to CSF absorption
Altered CSF production: controversial, may involve increased choroid plexus secretion
venousHypothesis: Primary or secondary venous sinus stenosis increases venous pressure
Increased venous pressure reduces CSF absorption pressure gradient across arachnoid granulations
Bilateral transverse sinus stenosis present in many IIH patients
Venous sinus stenting reduces pressure gradient and improves symptoms
Chicken-or-egg debate: venous stenosis may be cause or consequence of elevated ICP
obesityLink: Central obesity increases intra-abdominal and intrathoracic pressure
Elevated central venous pressure transmitted to cerebral venous system
Increased intrathoracic pressure impairs cerebral venous drainage
Weight loss reduces intra-abdominal pressure and improves CSF dynamics
Adipokines: leptin and other adipokines may influence CSF production or absorption
hormonalFactors: Estrogen and progesterone effects on CSF production and absorption
Polycystic ovary syndrome and hyperandrogenism association
opticNerveDamage: Elevated ICP is transmitted along optic nerve sheath to optic disc
Axoplasmic flow stasis at lamina cribrosa causes optic disc swelling
Chronic compression leads to axonal degeneration, optic atrophy, and irreversible visual loss
Retinal nerve fiber layer infarction from impaired perfusion

complications: Visual loss: most serious complication; irreversible optic atrophy from chronic papilledema; may progress to legal blindness in 10-25% without treatment
Optic atrophy: irreversible damage to optic nerve fibers; disc pallor on fundoscopy; permanent visual field defects and central vision loss
Cerebrospinal fluid leak: from LP (persistent post-LP headache, intracranial hypotension)
Surgical complications: shunt infection (10%), shunt obstruction (20-30% requiring revision), ONSF failure, venous sinus stent thrombosis or migration
Medical complications: acetazolamide-induced metabolic acidosis, hypokalemia, nephrolithiasis; topiramate cognitive impairment
Chronic daily headache: may persist even after ICP is normalized
Empty sella syndrome: progressive compression of pituitary gland; rarely causes endocrine dysfunction
Permanent visual field defects: peripheral field constriction even after ICP normalization
Cranial nerve palsy: most commonly abducens palsy; resolves with ICP reduction
mustNotMiss: Do NOT perform LP before ruling out mass lesion with appropriate imaging (MRI with contrast) if any concern
Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis can mimic IIH and requires MRV to exclude - do not miss this treatable and dangerous condition
Opening pressure must be measured in lateral decubitus position with legs extended - wrong position gives falsely elevated reading
Fulminant IIH with rapid vision loss is a neuro-ophthalmologic emergency requiring urgent surgical intervention, not gradual medical management
Weight loss is the most effective long-term treatment - not just medications
Monitor visual fields and OCT regularly - visual loss can occur without subjective symptoms
CSF must be sent for culture (including fungal and TB if risk factors) to exclude chronic meningitis
Acetazolamide is pregnancy category C - discuss risks/benefits in women of childbearing potential; alternatives limited
emergencyPriority: HIGH - Rapid vision loss in fulminant IIH is an emergency; otherwise urgent outpatient management with close monitoring
diagnosticClues: Obese young woman with daily headache, transient visual obscurations, and pulsatile tinnitus
Papilledema on fundoscopy: bilateral optic disc swelling with preserved visual acuity early
Opening pressure >250 mm CSF on lumbar puncture with normal CSF composition
Empty sella, flattened posterior globes, and optic nerve sheath distention on MRI
Enlarged blind spot on perimetry is classic
Headache worse in morning or when lying flat
Sixth nerve palsy (unilateral or bilateral) without other focal findings
exclusionClues: CSF pleocytosis (>5 WBC) or elevated protein indicates alternative diagnosis (meningitis, inflammatory disorder)
Ventricular enlargement on imaging indicates hydrocephalus, not IIH
Mass lesion on MRI excludes IIH by definition
Venous sinus thrombosis on MRV indicates secondary intracranial hypertension, not idiopathic
Opening pressure <250 mm CSF in lateral decubitus makes IIH unlikely
Absence of papilledema despite elevated ICP raises question of IIH without papilledema (controversial; requires careful exclusion)
Focal neurologic findings (other than abducens palsy) suggests alternative diagnosis
Severe visual loss at presentation without prior symptoms suggests alternative optic neuropathy
